calendar.matters:
MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Jan-29-2026 Vacated

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Jul-21-2025 Set For Trial On Apr-06-26 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

APPLICATION AND HEARING FOR RIGHT TO ATTACH ORDER
Off Calendar Apr-22-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
RE: PLAINTIFFS JOHN WILKINSON, AN INDIVIDUAL, MARCO DIGIULIO, MICHAEL MCLOUGHLIN, and PAT RONEY'S APPLICATION AND HEARING FOR RIGHT TO ATTACH ORDER.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person. The court adopts its tentative ruling. Plaintiffs John Wilkinson, Marco DiGiulio, Michael McLoughlin and Pat Roney's application for writ of attachment as to HCC 3, LLC is granted.

"Before an attachment order is issued, the court must find all of the following: (1) the claim upon which the attachment is based is one upon which an attachment may be issued; (2) the applicant has established 'the probable validity' of the claim upon which the attachment is based; (3) the attachment is not sought for a purpose other than the recovery on the claim upon which the request for attachment is based; and (4) the amount to be secured by the attachment is greater than zero. (Code of Civil Procedure section 484.090.) In order to establish the probable validity of its claim, the applicant must show that it is more likely than not it will obtain a judgment against the defendant (or counterdefendant) on its claim. (Code of Civil Procedure section 481.190.)" (Lydig Constr., Inc. v. Martinez Steel Corp. (2015) 234 Cal.App.4th 937, 944.) Plaintiffs must also show that the amount due is readily ascertainable. (Code of Civil Procedure section 483.010(a).)

Plaintiffs met this burden. Attachment may issue based on Plaintiffs' claim for breach of the Membership Interest Purchase Agreement ("MIPA"). Plaintiffs establish the probable validity of their breach of contract claim as HCC 3 failed to pay them. (Wilkinson Decl., pars. 5, 12-13.) The attachment is sought for a proper purpose and the amount to be secure is greater than zero. The amount due is readily ascertainable: Plaintiffs seek the three post-closing payments ($150,000), payments due under the "80/20 Program" ($163,176.34), and interest ($10,500). (Total = $173,676.34.)

In opposition, Defendant HCC, LLC relies on Code of Civil Procedure section 483.015(b)(2) and argues the court should deny attachment because Defendants have an offsetting claim for breach of contract that exceeds the value of Plaintiffs' claim. Specifically, Defendant argues that Plaintiffs breached the MIPA by accelerating the payment of accounts receivables to Defendants' detriment. This argument fails since Defendant fails to establish the probable validity of the offsetting claim. (Lydig Constr., Inc., 234 Cal.App.4th at 945.) Like the opposing party in Lydig, Defendant relies on a conclusory declaration. HCC 3's manager (Vogt) states that Plaintiffs violated Bin to Bottle's ordinary course of business by seeking payments from Naked Wines. Plaintiffs' objection to paragraph 8 of the Vogt declaration is sustained because the testimony lacks foundation as Vogt fails to explain a basis for making claims regarding Bin to Bottle's ordinary business practices. At most, Defendant has shown that Bin to Bottle did not always strictly seek to collect on its invoices. Wilkinson, the founder and former CEO of Bin to Bottle, explains that Bin to Bottle ordinarily would seek payments (even overdue payments) before the close of the year. (Wilkinson Reply Decl., pars. 6-9.) Plaintiffs' evidence there was no "acceleration" is more persuasive. "Courts are generally suspicious of vague, unsupported counterclaims and defenses." (Lydig Constr., Inc., 234 Cal.App.4th at 945.)

Plaintiffs shall post a $10,000 bond.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (302/JMQ)

APPLICATION AND HEARING FOR RIGHT TO ATTACH ORDER
Off Calendar Apr-22-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: PLAINTIFF JOHN WILKINSON , AN INDIVIDUAL, MARCO DIGIULIO, MICHAEL MCLOUGHLIN, PAT RONEY APPLICATION AND HEARING FOR RIGHT TO ATTAC.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person. The court adopts its tentative ruling. Plaintiff John Wilkinson's application for writ of attachment as to Bin to Bottle, LLC is granted.

"Before an attachment order is issued, the court must find all of the following: (1) the claim upon which the attachment is based is one upon which an attachment may be issued; (2) the applicant has established 'the probable validity' of the claim upon which the attachment is based; (3) the attachment is not sought for a purpose other than the recovery on the claim upon which the request for attachment is based; and (4) the amount to be secured by the attachment is greater than zero. (Code of Civil Procedure section 484.090.) In order to establish the probable validity of its claim, the applicant must show that it is more likely than not it will obtain a judgment against the defendant (or counterdefendant) on its claim. (Sec. 481.190.)" (Lydig Constr., Inc. v. Martinez Steel Corp. (2015) 234 Cal.App.4th 937, 944.) Plaintiffs must also show that the amount due is readily ascertainable. (Code of Civil Procedure section 483.010(a).)

Plaintiff Wilkinson met his burden. Attachment may issue based on Wilkinson's claim for breach of the Consulting Agreement. Wilkinson establishes the probable validity of his breach of contract claim as Bin to Bottle failed to pay him. (Wilkinson Decl., par. 16) The attachment is sought for a proper purpose and the amount to be secure is greater than zero. The amount due is readily ascertainable. Bin to Bottle owes $25,000 for its breach. (Wilkinson Decl., pars. 14-15.)

In opposition, Bin to Bottle relies on the same offset argument that it raised in the companion motion. Wilkinson argues that the argument fails because Bin to Bottle was not a party to Membership Interest Purchase Agreement ("MIPA"). That assertion is inaccurate as it is a named party. In any event, the court grants Wilkinson's motion because as explained in the companion motion, the offset argument lacks merit.

Wilkinson shall post an undertaking of $10,000.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (302/JMQ)
